annotations.label: Mathematical induction

anchor.snippet: Mathematical induction is a method for proving that a statement

annotations.note: The principle of mathematical induction on ℕ is built into Lean as Nat.rec; Mathlib's Nat.le_induction in Mathlib.Data.Nat.Init packages the usual 'base + step' form.

annotations.label: Base case and induction step

anchor.snippet: A proof by induction consists of two cases.

annotations.note: Nat.le_induction in Mathlib.Data.Nat.Init explicitly takes a base case and a successor (induction) step.

annotations.label: Structural induction

anchor.snippet: this generalization, known as structural induction

annotations.note: Structural induction is realized via Lean's auto-generated .rec principles and WellFounded.induction (Init.WF, re-exported by Mathlib.Order.WellFounded).

annotations.label: Simplest form of mathematical induction

anchor.snippet: The simplest and most common form of mathematical induction infers that a statement involving a natural number

annotations.note: Nat.le_induction encodes the standard P(0) ∧ (∀ n, P n → P (n+1)) → ∀ n, P n schema.

annotations.label: Induction hypothesis

anchor.snippet: The hypothesis in the induction step, that the statement holds for a particular

annotations.note: The induction hypothesis is a meta-mathematical notion; in Mathlib it appears as the recursive argument of induction principles such as Nat.le_induction, not as a separate definition.

annotations.label: Sum of consecutive natural numbers formula

anchor.snippet: Proposition. For every

annotations.note: Finset.sum_range_id gives ∑ i ∈ range n, i = n(n-1)/2, the standard Gauss summation formula.

annotations.label: Proof of sum formula by induction

anchor.snippet: We give a proof by induction on

annotations.note: The lemma Finset.sum_range_id is established in Mathlib; the inductive proof is internal to that file.

annotations.label: Trigonometric inequality |sin nx| ≤ n|sin x|

anchor.snippet: Proposition. For any

annotations.note: Loogle for the conclusion pattern and grep of Mathlib's trigonometry files turned up |sin x| ≤ |x| (Real.abs_sin_le_abs) and Lipschitz bounds but no |sin (n·x)| ≤ n |sin x| statement.

annotations.label: Proof of trigonometric inequality

anchor.snippet: Fix an arbitrary real number

annotations.note: No proof of |sin nx| ≤ n |sin x| located in Mathlib's trigonometry files.

annotations.label: Induction with base case b

anchor.snippet: If one wishes to prove a statement, not for all natural numbers, but only for all numbers

annotations.note: Nat.le_induction proves ∀ n ≥ b, P n from P b and the successor step, matching induction with arbitrary base.

annotations.label: Forming dollar amounts by 4- and 5-dollar coins

anchor.section: Example: forming dollar amounts by coins
anchor.snippet: Assume an infinite supply of 4- and 5-dollar coins.

annotations.note: No Frobenius/Chicken-McNugget statement for the {4,5} numerical semigroup found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Induction on more than one counter

anchor.section: Induction on more than one counter
anchor.snippet: It is sometimes desirable to prove a statement involving two natural numbers

annotations.note: Double induction is realized via product/lexicographic well-foundedness; Prod.Lex.instIsWellFounded in Mathlib/Order/RelClasses.lean gives the well-foundedness needed for nested induction on two counters.

annotations.label: Method of infinite descent

anchor.snippet: The method of infinite descent is a variation of mathematical induction

annotations.note: Mathlib has WellFounded.induction_bot in Mathlib/Order/WellFounded.lean capturing the descent principle, but no decl named 'infinite descent' was found.

annotations.label: Limited mathematical induction

anchor.snippet: If one wishes to prove that a property

annotations.note: Bounded/limited induction (induction up to a fixed bound, a proof-theoretic notion) was not located in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prefix induction

anchor.snippet: A variant of interest in computational complexity is "prefix induction"

annotations.note: Prefix induction is a complexity-theoretic notion (induction on binary length) not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: A variant of interest in computational complexity is "prefix induction"

annotations.label: Complete (strong) induction

anchor.snippet: Another variant, called complete induction

annotations.note: Nat.strong_induction_on (and Nat.strongRecOn') in Mathlib/Data/Nat/Init.lean is the standard complete-induction principle on ℕ.

annotations.label: Equivalence of complete and ordinary induction

anchor.snippet: Complete induction is equivalent to ordinary mathematical induction as described above

annotations.note: Both principles are theorems in Mathlib (derived from Nat.rec), but no explicit equivalence statement between them was found.

annotations.label: Fibonacci numbers via complete induction

anchor.snippet: Complete induction is most useful when several instances of the inductive hypothesis are required

annotations.note: Mathlib defines Nat.fib by ordinary recursion and proves many lemmas using induction, but there is no headline example labelled 'Fibonacci via strong induction'.

anchors.snippet: Complete induction is most useful when several instances of the inductive hypothesis are required

annotations.label: Prime factorization via complete induction

anchor.snippet: Another proof by complete induction uses the hypothesis that the statement holds for all smaller

annotations.note: The existence of a prime divisor (proved by strong induction) is Nat.exists_prime_and_dvd; the full UFD statement is in Mathlib.RingTheory.UniqueFactorizationDomain.

annotations.label: Dollar amounts via strong induction

anchor.snippet: We shall look to prove the same example as above

annotations.note: No formal statement of the {4,5}-coin representation example was located in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: We shall look to prove the same example as above

annotations.label: Forward-backward induction

anchor.snippet: Sometimes, it is more convenient to deduce backwards

annotations.note: Nat.decreasingInduction in Mathlib/Data/Nat/Init.lean provides downward induction, the 'backward' half of Cauchy's forward-backward scheme.

annotations.label: All horses are the same color (faulty induction)

anchor.section: Example of error in the induction step
anchor.snippet: Joel E. Cohen proposed the following argument, which purports to prove by mathematical induction that all horses are of the same color

annotations.note: Cohen's all-horses-same-color paradox is a pedagogical example not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Second-order axiom of induction

anchor.snippet: In second-order logic , one can write down the " axiom of induction" as follows:

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize second-order logic or its axiom of induction as a syntactic object.

anchors.snippet: In second-order logic , one can write down the " axiom of induction" as follows:

annotations.label: Peano's axioms for the natural numbers

anchor.snippet: The axiom of structural induction for the natural numbers was first formulated by Peano

annotations.note: ℕ is built into Lean's core (not via an axiomatic Peano system); no PeanoAxioms structure was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Induction in ZFC set theory

anchor.snippet: In first-order ZFC set theory , quantification over predicates is not allowed, but one can still express induction by quantification over sets:

annotations.note: Mathlib is based on dependent type theory, not ZFC, and does not state induction as a ZFC set-quantifier schema.

anchors.snippet: In first-order ZFC set theory , quantification over predicates is not allowed, but one can still express induction by quantification over sets:

anchors.value: {\displaystyle \forall A{\Bigl (}0\in A\land \forall k\in \mathbb {N} {\bigl (}k\in A\to (k+1)\in A{\bigr )}\to \mathbb {N} \subseteq A{\Bigr )}

annotations.label: Transfinite induction

anchor.snippet: One variation of the principle of complete induction can be generalized for statements about elements of any well-founded set

annotations.note: WellFounded.induction (Init.WF, re-exported in Mathlib.Order.WellFounded) provides the transfinite/well-founded induction principle.

annotations.label: Induction implies well-ordering principle

anchor.snippet: It can then be proved that induction, given the above-listed axioms, implies the well-ordering principle.

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the equivalence well-founded ↔ every nonempty set has a minimum element (WellFounded.wellFounded_iff_has_min), implicitly the natural-number equivalence between induction and well-ordering.

annotations.label: Lexicographically well-ordered set violating induction

anchor.snippet: On the other hand, the set

annotations.note: No counterexample illustrating successor-induction failure on a non-ω well-ordered set was located in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sum of first n odd integers equals n²

anchor.snippet: the sum of the first n odd integers is n

annotations.note: Maurolico's identity ∑_{k<n} (2k+1) = n² follows from Finset.sum_range_id_mul_two; no headline lemma named for this identity was located.

annotations.label: AM-GM inequality (Cauchy's forward-backward proof)

anchor.snippet: the inequality of arithmetic and geometric means

annotations.note: The weighted AM-GM inequality is Real.geom_mean_le_arith_mean_weighted in Mathlib.Analysis.MeanInequalities; Cauchy's specific forward-backward induction proof is not separately formalized.
